locked_facts: Simple microscope (magnifying glass): M = 1 + D/f (image at D); M = D/f (image at infinity)
Compound microscope: M = (L/f_o)(D/f_e) for image at D; M = (L/f_o)(D/f_e) approximately when L >> f_o, f_e
Astronomical telescope (refracting): M = f_o/f_e (normal adjustment, image at infinity)
Telescope tube length in normal adjustment: L = f_o + f_e
Resolving power of telescope ∝ diameter of objective (larger aperture resolves finer details)
Reflecting telescope (Cassegrain): uses concave primary + convex secondary mirror — avoids chromatic aberration
truth_statements: A simple microscope is a convex lens of short focal length used to see a magnified virtual image of a nearby object, with magnifying power M = 1 + D/f
A compound microscope uses two convex lenses — a short focal length objective that forms a real magnified image, and an eyepiece that acts as a simple microscope to magnify that image further

An astronomical telescope uses a long focal length objective to form a real image of a distant object at its focus, and a short focal length eyepiece to magnify that image — the magnifying power is f_o/f_e
In normal adjustment of a telescope, the final image is at infinity and the tube length equals f_o + f_e
The resolving power of a telescope depends on the diameter of the objective lens — larger objectives can distinguish finer angular details

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show a compound microscope: the tiny object is just beyond f_o of the objective. The objective creates a real, magnified, inverted intermediate image inside the tube. This intermediate image falls within f_e of the eyepiece, which acts as a magnifying glass to produce a final virtual, highly magnified image. Total magnification = (magnification by objective) × (magnification by eyepiece). Now contrast with a telescope: the objective has a LONG focal length (to collect light from distant objects) and the eyepiece has a SHORT focal length (to magnify the small intermediate image). The roles of f_o and f_e are reversed!
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Draw both instruments side by side. In the microscope: f_o is tiny, f_e is small. In the telescope: f_o is large, f_e is small. Highlight that the microscope multiplies both magnifications, while the telescope's magnifying power is simply f_o/f_e.

simulation_states.label: Simple microscope — magnifying glass
simulation_states.description: A convex lens of short focal length (f = 5 cm). A small object is placed between f and the lens. The lens produces a virtual, erect, magnified image at the near point (D = 25 cm). M = 1 + D/f = 1 + 25/5 = 6.
simulation_states.emphasis: The simplest optical instrument: a single convex lens acting as a magnifying glass. The object must be within the focal length for a virtual magnified image.

simulation_states.label: Compound microscope — two-stage magnification
simulation_states.description: Objective lens (f_o = 1 cm) and eyepiece (f_e = 5 cm) separated by tube length L = 15 cm. Object placed just beyond f_o. Objective forms a real, magnified, inverted intermediate image. Eyepiece magnifies this image further. Total M = (L/f_o)(D/f_e) ≈ 75.
simulation_states.emphasis: Two-stage magnification: the objective magnifies the object into an intermediate real image, then the eyepiece magnifies that image. Both must be convex lenses with short focal lengths.

simulation_states.label: Astronomical telescope — distant objects magnified
simulation_states.description: Objective (f_o = 100 cm, large diameter) and eyepiece (f_e = 5 cm). Parallel rays from a distant star converge at the focal plane of the objective, forming a tiny real image. Eyepiece magnifies this image. M = f_o/f_e = 100/5 = 20. Tube length = f_o + f_e = 105 cm.
simulation_states.emphasis: The telescope's objective has a LONG focal length and LARGE diameter — long f_o gives high magnification (M = f_o/f_e), large diameter gives high resolving power and light-gathering ability. The final image is inverted.

simulation_states.label: Interactive — compare instruments
simulation_states.description: Sliders to change f_o, f_e, and tube length. Switch between microscope and telescope modes. Watch magnification update live. Compare how changing each focal length affects magnification differently in each instrument.
simulation_states.emphasis: For the microscope: both smaller f_o and smaller f_e increase magnification. For the telescope: larger f_o and smaller f_e increase magnification. Try extreme values and see practical limits.

common_misconceptions.belief: A telescope magnifies objects by making them physically larger — like zooming in
trigger_phrases: telescope se cheezein badi hoti hain
common_misconceptions.correction: A telescope does not make objects physically larger. It increases the angle subtended by the object at the eye (angular magnification). A distant star that subtends 0.01° to the naked eye might subtend 0.2° through a telescope — it appears larger because it spans a bigger angle on your retina, but the star itself is unchanged.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the angular size concept: object far away subtends small angle → objective collects these rays and creates an intermediate image → eyepiece magnifies the angle. Label the angular magnification M = angle with telescope / angle without.

common_misconceptions.belief: The eyepiece of a telescope should have a large focal length for more magnification
trigger_phrases: eyepiece ka focal length bada hona chahiye
common_misconceptions.correction: For a telescope, M = f_o/f_e — the eyepiece focal length is in the DENOMINATOR. A SMALLER f_e gives MORE magnification. The objective should have a LARGE focal length (and large diameter for light gathering), but the eyepiece should have a SHORT focal length to maximize magnification.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show f_e slider: as f_e decreases, the magnification M = f_o/f_e increases. Contrast with the microscope where the eyepiece magnification is D/f_e — in both cases, smaller f_e gives more magnification.

common_misconceptions.belief: A microscope and telescope work on the same principle — they are basically the same instrument
trigger_phrases: dono ek hi tarah kaam karte hain
common_misconceptions.correction: Although both use two convex lenses, they are designed for opposite purposes. A microscope has a very short f_o (to magnify tiny nearby objects) and M = (L/f_o)(D/f_e). A telescope has a very long f_o (to view distant objects with small angular size) and M = f_o/f_e. The objective of a microscope is small; the objective of a telescope is large (for light gathering).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show both instruments side by side with their ray diagrams. Highlight the different f_o values: microscope f_o ≈ 1 cm vs telescope f_o ≈ 100 cm. Show that the magnification formulas are different.

legend.meaning: Least distance of distinct vision = 25 cm
Tube length (distance between objective and eyepiece)
Ability to distinguish closely spaced objects — depends on aperture
